title: A/B Testing Prompt Variants
concept: Comparing two prompt phrasings head-to-head on the same eval set with a clear win metric.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we learned about building an 'eval set'. What is its main eval set'. What is it?

options.text: A list of randomly generated passwords.
A curated set of representative inputs used to test prompts for regressions.
The training data used to build the foundational LLM.

exercises.explain: An eval set is a curated collection of representative inputs and target outputs used to test prompt performance consistently.

cards.title: The Battle of the Prompts
cards.body: When you tweak a prompt to fix one error, you might accidentally break three other things. How do you prove that your new prompt is actually better than your old one without just guessing?

cards.title: What is Prompt A/B Testing?
cards.body: A/B testing prompts means running two slightly different versions (A and B) against the exact same test inputs. We then use a strict win metric, like accuracy or response length, to decide which version performs better.

cards.title: The Taste Test analogy
cards.body: Think of prompt A/B testing like a taste test. You don't guess which recipe is better by tasting it once; you serve both recipe A and recipe B to multiple people (your eval set) and count who gets the most empty plates (your win metric).

cards.title: Putting it to the Test
cards.body: A support team runs Prompt A ('Answer the customer') and Prompt B ('Answer politely in 2 sentences') against 50 saved customer questions. They measure success by counting how many times the output is actually under 2 sentences.

cards.title: Don't Trust Your Eyeballs
cards.body: You might think you can just eyeball a few outputs from Prompt A and B to pick a winner. In reality, human intuition is highly biased; you need a consistent metric applied to the whole eval set to find the true champion.

cards.title: Ad-hoc vs. Scientific Testing
cards.body: Compare how a structured A/B test differs from disorganized, random prompt tweaking.

left.text: Changing prompts randomly and testing on different inputs.

right.text: Comparing Prompt A vs B on the exact same eval set with one win metric.

exercises.prompt: What must remain constant when comparing Prompt A and Prompt B head-to-head?

options.text: A set of completely different questions for each prompt.
The exact same eval set of test cases.
A metric that changes during the test.

exercises.explain: An eval set must remain identical for both versions of the prompt to ensure a fair, apples-to-apples comparison.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A/B testing prompts requires defining a clear win metric before comparing outputs.
exercises.explain: A/B testing requires a clear, measurable win metric (like 'formatted as valid JSON' or 'under 100 words') rather than just vague feelings.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to complete the sentence on how we measure prompt performance.
exercises.sentence: To declare a clear winner between two prompt variants, we must measure them using a consistent win metric across the entire test suite.
exercises.explain: A win metric is the quantitative rule (like accuracy rate or character limit pass rate) you use to decide which prompt variant performed better.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes an automated AI-assisted evaluation run for prompt testing?

options.text: A developer reads through 10 outputs and manually rates them on a sticky note.
Showing the prompt outputs to random users and asking for feedback thumbs-up.
An LLM evaluator grades the outputs of Prompt A and Prompt B using a structured grading rubric.

exercises.explain: Option C represents an automated, scaleable AI evaluation process using a structured rubric to grade prompt outputs. Options A and B are manual human checks.

exercises.prompt: You want to test if adding 'Explain step-by-step' to your prompt improves accuracy. What is the correct way to set up this A/B test?

options.text: Run Prompt A on the old eval set and Prompt B on a new, harder set.
Run both Prompt A and B on the same eval set and compare them using the same win metric.
Ask a single developer to read one output from each prompt and pick their favorite.
Deploy Prompt B immediately to all users and see if anyone complains.

exercises.explain: To declare a winner, you must run both prompt variants against the identical eval set and measure them using the exact same success metric. Changing the eval set midway introduces bias and ruins the test.
